judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports independently hit all five expectations with comparable specificity and accurate citations; Report 2 adds slightly more detail (e.g., full commit hash, missing CI workflow observation) but substantively they are equivalent.

report_1.evidence: Postgres row-level security has a built-in exception: the owner of a table always bypasses RLS on that table unless the table is also marked FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY. This repo never issues a FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY statement anywhere... The application's runtime DATABASE_URL currently points at juniper_owner, not juniper_app: docker-compose.yml:5 ... .env.example:1 ... infra/main.tf:1-3

report_2.evidence: the Postgres exempts the owning role of a table from its own RLS policies by default, independently of the BYPASSRLS role attribute — ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY alone does not apply to the owner; only ALTER TABLE ... FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY would... in the current deployment configuration, DATABASE_URL is set to juniper_owner, not juniper_app: .env.example:1 ... docker-compose.yml:5 ... infra/main.tf:2

report_1.evidence: Commit b2a23d2 ("ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot", authored Wed Jun 17 2026) is the exact change. ... -DATABASE_URL=postgresql://juniper_app:apppw@db:5432/juniper +DATABASE_URL=postgresql://juniper_owner:ownerpw@db:5432/juniper ... applied identically to .env.example, docker-compose.yml, and infra/main.tf.

report_2.evidence: Commit b2a23d2a527cd13589163a40ef51b2eb2a430af6, "ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot", 2026-06-17 15:20:00 +0000. It changed all three deployment surfaces from juniper_app to juniper_owner: .env.example: juniper_app:apppw -> juniper_owner:ownerpw docker-compose.yml: ... infra/main.tf: ...

report_1.evidence: Primary fix — stop serving requests as the table owner. Revert docker-compose.yml:5, .env.example:1, and infra/main.tf:1-3 to use juniper_app for the runtime DATABASE_URL... Defense in depth at the DB layer. Add ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; ... in a new migration

report_2.evidence: Primary fix — stop the runtime from ever connecting as the owner. Revert .env.example:1, docker-compose.yml:5, and infra/main.tf:2 back to juniper_app:apppw@... ... Defense in depth — force RLS even for the owner. Add ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; to a new migration

report_1.evidence: Defense in depth at the app layer. Add an explicit WHERE tenant_id = :tenant_id to the query in backend/app/routes/appointments.py:16-19, bound from claims["tenant_id"].

report_2.evidence: Also worth adding a WHERE tenant_id = :tid clause in backend/app/routes/appointments.py:16-19 as a second independent layer, since currently RLS is the only thing scoping that query.

report_1.evidence: A startup assertion in backend/app/db.py/app lifespan that queries SELECT current_user, (SELECT rolname FROM pg_tables JOIN pg_roles ON tableowner = rolname WHERE tablename = 'appointments') (or equivalent) and refuses to boot if the runtime role equals the table owner — turns this misconfiguration into an immediate boot-time failure instead of a silent data leak.

report_2.evidence: Add a boot-time assertion in the API startup path that queries pg_roles/has_table_privilege (or simply SELECT current_user) and hard-fails startup if the connected role is a superuser, has BYPASSRLS, or — critically here — is the owner of appointments/tenants... Treat any positive result as fatal, not a warning
